answers.field_label: Present Land Use Zoning General Plan Designation
answers.value: The approximately 68.5-acre project site is currently in unincorporated Sutter County but within the City of Riverbend sphere of influence. Existing land use consists primarily of irrigated row crops (alfalfa and seasonal vegetables) with a small cluster of aging agricultural outbuildings near the northwest corner. There are no existing residences on the site. The Sutter County zoning designation is AG-20 (General Agriculture, 20-acre minimum). The City of Riverbend General Plan designates the area as Mixed Use Neighborhood (MUN) with a target residential density of 8–18 dwelling units per acre and supporting neighborhood-serving commercial uses along South River Road. Surrounding land uses include single-family residential neighborhoods to the north, the Feather River and associated riparian corridor to the east, active agricultural fields to the south, and a mix of light industrial and commercial uses along State Route 99 to the west.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project proposes to annex and comprehensively develop approximately 68.5 acres of currently agricultural land at the southern edge of the City of Riverbend into a walkable, transit-supportive neighborhood that combines residential, neighborhood-serving commercial, and office uses with extensive open space and recreational amenities.

Residential Development (Approx. 32.4 acres, 312 units):
The project includes a diverse mix of housing types intended to serve a range of household sizes and income levels. This includes approximately 146 single-family detached homes on small lots (average 4,000–5,000 square feet), 96 townhome units arranged in clusters of four to six units with shared motor courts, and a 70-unit, three-story apartment building located near the transit plaza and neighborhood commercial center. The apartment building will include a mix of one-, two-, and three-bedroom units, with 14 units (20 percent) reserved as deed-restricted affordable housing targeting low- and moderate-income households. Residential areas are organized around a central green and neighborhood parks, with an internal network of sidewalks and Class I multi-use paths that prioritize pedestrian and bicycle movement over vehicle traffic.

Commercial and Office Uses (Approx. 16.3 acres, 205,000 sq ft):
Along South River Road, the project proposes a neighborhood commercial center with approximately 120,000 square feet of commercial uses, including a small-format grocery store (approximately 35,000 square feet), a pharmacy, cafés, local restaurants, a daycare center, and various service-oriented retail spaces (e.g., dry cleaner, fitness studio, and a credit union branch). The commercial buildings are designed with active street frontages, outdoor dining areas, and shaded plazas to encourage walkability and community gathering.

Behind the commercial frontage, the project includes approximately 85,000 square feet of two- to three-story multi-tenant office space targeted toward medical, professional, and small technology or creative firms. The office component is expected to accommodate approximately 280 daytime employees, which will help balance local jobs and housing and reduce commuting distances for Riverbend residents.

Parks, Open Space, and Recreational Amenities (Approx. 6.0 acres plus linear open space):
The project features a central 2.5-acre neighborhood park with a children’s playground, a small amphitheater with terraced seating for community events and outdoor movie nights, a half-court basketball area, pickleball courts, and shaded picnic areas with BBQs. A 1.5-acre pocket park is located near the townhomes, providing a tot lot, community garden plots, and a small dog park separated for large and small dogs. Another 2.0-acre community green lies adjacent to the apartment building and transit plaza, offering flexible turf space for informal sports, pop-up markets, and seasonal events.

Along the eastern boundary of the site, adjacent to Willow Creek, the project will establish a linear riparian open space corridor with a multi-use trail connecting to the City’s existing trail network. This corridor will incorporate habitat restoration measures such as planting native riparian vegetation, installing interpretive signage, and providing overlook areas with benches and educational displays about local wildlife and the history of agriculture in the Riverbend area.

Transportation and Circulation Improvements:
The project will construct a new internal street network consisting of two primary east–west collector streets and several local residential streets designed with traffic-calming features such as narrow travel lanes, curb extensions, and roundabouts at two key intersections. Class II bike lanes will be installed on the collectors, and sidewalks will be provided on both sides of all streets. A new signalized intersection will be constructed at South River Road and Willow Creek Drive to provide safe access into the project site, with dedicated turn lanes and upgraded pedestrian crosswalks.

A small transit plaza will be developed near the commercial center and apartment building, including covered bus shelters, real-time transit information displays, bicycle racks, and space for future micro-mobility options (e.g., bike share or scooter share). The project will coordinate with the Riverbend Transit Agency to extend an existing local bus route into the site, providing direct service to downtown Riverbend, Riverbend Community College, and major employment centers.

Infrastructure, Utilities, and Water Facilities:
The project will connect to the City’s existing municipal water system via a new 12-inch water main extension along South River Road. On-site water distribution lines will loop through the development to ensure redundancy and adequate fire flow. Estimated average potable water demand is approximately 0.35 MGD at full buildout. Sanitary sewer flows will be conveyed via new gravity sewer lines to an existing trunk main in Willow Creek Drive, ultimately discharging to the City’s wastewater treatment plant. Preliminary analysis indicates that the plant has sufficient permitted capacity to accommodate project flows with minor off-site collection system improvements.

Stormwater management will rely on a combination of low-impact development (LID) features and dual-purpose stormwater quality and detention basins. Bioswales and bioretention planters will be integrated into street parkways and parking lot medians to capture and treat runoff at the source. Two primary detention basins located at the southern and eastern edges of the site will be landscaped and accessible as informal open space, with walking paths and native plantings. The project is designed to maintain post-development peak runoff rates at or below existing conditions for the 10-year and 100-year storm events.

Phasing and Construction:
Buildout is anticipated over a 6- to 8-year period in three main phases, beginning with backbone infrastructure (water, sewer, storm, and primary roads) and the first residential neighborhood, followed by the neighborhood commercial center and office buildings, and concluding with the apartment building and final residential clusters. Construction activities will generally occur between 7:00 a.m. and 7:00 p.m. on weekdays and 8:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m. on Saturdays, with no routine work on Sundays or City-recognized holidays. The Draft EIR evaluates construction-related air quality, noise, traffic, and biological impacts and identifies mitigation measures such as dust control, construction equipment mufflers, timing restrictions near sensitive receptors, and pre-construction nesting bird surveys.

Objectives and Community Benefits:
Key project objectives include: (1) implementing the City of Riverbend General Plan Mixed Use Neighborhood land use designation by providing a compact, walkable community that reduces reliance on single-occupancy vehicles; (2) expanding the City’s housing supply with a variety of unit types, including deed-restricted affordable units; (3) creating local employment opportunities in neighborhood-serving retail and office uses; (4) enhancing public access to open space and the Willow Creek riparian corridor; and (5) incorporating sustainable design principles such as LID stormwater features, shade trees, and energy-efficient building design. The project proponent has also committed to working with a local nonprofit to program the community amphitheater and central green with regular family-friendly events, farmers’ markets, and cultural festivals, fostering a strong sense of place and community identity.

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is intended to serve as a model for context-sensitive growth at the city’s edge by blending higher-density housing, jobs, services, and recreation in a manner that is compatible with adjacent neighborhoods and protective of nearby natural resources.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form
